full_response:
For a Schwarzschild line element  

ds² = −(1−2GM/r) dt² + (1−2GM/r)⁻¹ dr² + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²) ,

consider the world-line  

r = r₀ , θ = θ₀ , φ = φ₀ , t = t(λ) ,

and, as given in the problem, take the tangent vector in these coordinates to be  

U^μ = (1 , 0 , 0 , 0) .            (1)

1.  Contraction with the metric  
   U^a U_a = g_{tt} (U^t)² = [ −(1−2GM/r₀) ] (1)²  
             = −(1−2GM/r₀) .                               (2)

   Except at r₀ → ∞, the right–hand side is not −1, so (1) does **not** satisfy U·U = −1.

2.  Why the discrepancy?  
   • A true 4-velocity must be defined with respect to proper time τ:  
           U^μ ≡ dx^μ / dτ .  
   • In (1) we implicitly used the coordinate time t as the parameter (λ = t), not the proper time.  
   • Along this world-line the proper time is related to t by  
           dτ² = (1−2GM/r₀) dt² ⇒ dτ = √(1−2GM/r₀) dt .  
   • Hence the physical 4-velocity for the static observer is  

           U^μ_phys = dx^μ/dτ = (dt/dτ , 0 , 0 , 0)  
                      = (1/√(1−2GM/r₀) , 0 , 0 , 0) .      (3)

   Contracting (3) with the metric indeed gives U·U = −1.

Therefore the vector written in the statement is merely the coordinate basis vector ∂/∂t; it must be rescaled by 1/√(1−2GM/r₀) to become a properly normalized 4-velocity.

\boxed{\text{No.  With U^\mu=(1,0,0,0) we obtain }U^a U_a=-(1-2GM/r_0)\neq -1;\text{ the mismatch arises because the curve was parametrized by the coordinate time }t\text{ rather than by the proper time }\tau. \text{ Multiplying by }(1-2GM/r_0)^{-1/2} yields the correctly normalized 4-velocity.}}

Confidence: 92%
extracted_answer: \text{No.  With U^\mu=(1,0,0,0) we obtain }U^a U_a=-(1-2GM/r_0)\neq -1;\text{ the mismatch arises because the curve was parametrized by the coordinate time }t\text{ rather than by the proper time }\tau. \text{ Multiplying by }(1-2GM/r_0)^{-1/2} yields the correctly normalized 4-velocity.
